FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (N=7514; ages 9-10 at baseline; 2016-2018)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (hours per day, self-reported) is a qualifying variable per rule B1; also includes problematic social media use measured by SMAQ (Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale). Both are qualifying SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with RR=1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43, p<0.001) for SM time → alcohol sipping; mediation analysis testing indirect effect through problematic SM use (BC 95% CI 0.007-0.016, p<0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Main text Table 2 and Table 3 report RR (95% CI), p-values, and mediation proportions with 95% CI.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis examining prospective association between SM time and alcohol sipping outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: No indication of duplicate; original empirical research article.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not qualify for FT-EC7. SM is the independent/predictor variable (Year 1 SM time predicting Year 3 outcome), not the dependent variable. There is SM → outcome analysis (alcohol sipping), violating condition (a). Fails FT-EC7 binding rule B18.
notes: Three-year prospective design. Social media time is the independent variable predicting alcohol sipping as dependent outcome, with mediation analysis examining problematic SM use as mediator. No demographic-only predictors of SM.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (N=4,718 U.S. adolescents aged 10-15 years between 2018 and 2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as total recreational screen time hours per day (self-reported). No breakdown of platform or app-specific use reported. Not a qualifying SM variable per B7 (unspecified platform). No separate SM, texting, video chat, or app-category coefficient reported. Fails rule B2: only summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression with continuous and categorical screen time and steps; Table 2 reports B (95% CI) and p-values (e.g., continuous screen time B=0.27, p=0.011).

FT_IC4.evidence: Main text Table 2 and Table 1 report B coefficients with 95% CI and p-values for screen time and CVD risk factors.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD Study analysis examining screen time and physical activity associations with CVD risk factors.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; original research article.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable predicting CVD outcomes (blood pressure, cholesterol, hemoglobin A1c), not SM as dependent variable. Does not meet criteria for FT-EC7; SM is IV, not DV.
notes: Cross-sectional analysis (Years 2-3 data). Screen time is general unspecified platform use, not qualifying SM. Only total screen time reported, no per-item breakdown of SM, texting, video chat, or app use. Fails FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study (N=9,538 adolescents, 9-10 years at baseline in 2016-2018, with two years follow-up)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time includes multiple qualifying variables: texting, video chat, videos (YouTube), and video games as separate items per rule B1. These specific screen types are reported with separate coefficients in abstract and text (B=0.10, 0.07, 0.06, 0.06 for depressive symptoms). Texting and video chat are qualifying SM/smartphone variables per coder prompt.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models with random intercepts; regression coefficients reported (e.g., depressive symptoms B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06-0.13, p<0.001 for total screen time; specific screen types in Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Main text and Table 2 report B coefficients (95% CI) and p-values for baseline screen time predicting mental health symptoms at one and two-year follow-up.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD Study prospective analysis examining baseline screen time associations with follow-up mental health outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; original longitudinal research article.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including texting and video chat as SM/smartphone exposures) is the independent variable predicting mental health outcomes, not SM as dependent variable. Does not qualify for FT-EC7; SM is IV, not DV.
notes: Two-year prospective design (baseline to Year 2 follow-up). Multiple qualifying screen types reported separately with own coefficients: texting, video chat, videos, and video games. Child Behavior Checklist DSM-oriented scales (depression, anxiety, somatic, ADHD, conduct, ODD) are outcomes. Specific screen types with greatest associations: video chat, texting, videos, video games for depressive symptoms.
